# Conclusion

This report analysed how the configuration of green relates to pluvial flooding in Delft and Xi’an. The project used a shared spatial workflow based on green space metrics, FWEI derived flood indicators, DEM variables, correlation analysis, and combined typology construction. Both cities were studied within the same 10 km by 10 km study area size, which made it possible to compare two very different urban contexts using the same method.

The main finding is that in both Delft and Xi’an, the strongest FWEI derived surface water values were found in Type 3, a combined typology class with relatively high green coverage and strong green space contiguity. This means that the relationship between green space configuration and surface water is more complex than a simple “more green means less flooding” assumption.

Delft and Xi’an also showed different spatial structures. Delft had a more mixed pattern, with a large moderate background condition and a smaller but clearer green high water condition. Xi’an was more strongly dominated by a widespread background condition with fragmented detected surface water patches, while the strongest surface water values were concentrated in a smaller green and contiguous area. This shows that the same shared typology can identify comparable patterns, but that the dominant urban conditions still differ between the two cities.

These findings should be read as spatial associations based on FWEI derived surface water change, not as direct measurements of flood depth or flood risk. Still, the project shows that combined typologies can help identify where green space configuration and detected surface water patterns overlap. In both Delft and Xi’an, this overlap was strongest in greener and more connected areas.
